dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for Stab/Gunshot Wound/Penetrating Trauma.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
A 27-year-old male was shot in his kitchen, about 10 minutes ago.
He's alert and oriented. Can you tell me your name?
Uh, my name is... Jonathan.
Where are we right now, Jonathan?
In my... kitchen.
Do you know what day it is?
I think... it's Tuesday.
Alright, can you tell me what happened just now?
Someone shot me... I'm not sure who.
Eyes are open and responding. Pupils appear equal and round, 3 mm.
Airway is patent, and he is breathing on his own. Any trouble with your breathing?
Maybe, but it's hard to describe.
Respirations are rapid-28 per minute. I notice chest symmetry and clear, equal bilateral breath sounds with the exception of the gun shot wound to the left lower chest.
Heart rate is 123, regular. Blood pressure is 139 over 95. We need to address the wounds now. Skin is pink, warm, and clammy with normal turgor.
Initial BP recorded as 139/95 mmHg.
Pulse is 123, strong and regular on palpation at the radial artery.
Rate of 28 breaths per minute observed and regularly timed.
Current oxygen saturation is 97% using pulse oximetry.
EKG from monitoring shows sinus tachycardia with no arrhythmias at this time.
Glucose is not immediately available with the equipment we have.
I noted multiple gunshot wounds. Focusing on securing the wound in his left upper leg first. No signs of pulselessness or pale extremities noted.
Tourniquet applied to the left upper leg-bleeding is now controlled.
Applied petroleum gauze over the left lower chest injury as an occlusive dressing.
IV started in an extremity vein, cannula secured and flowing.
Updated pulse is 88, smooth and strong.
Respirations increased to 32 per minute. Work of breathing seems higher now.
BP now is 160/67 with an increase in systolic.
Oximetry remains at 97%. Still no need for supplemental oxygen at this time.
EKG transitioned from tachycardia to normal sinus rhythm per manual interpretation.
Did you experience a loss of consciousness today?
No, just the generalized pain from the injuries.
Any medications or blood thinners you're taking regularly?
No, I don't take any.
Any known allergies?
I don't think so. I can't recall.
Rate the pain from 0 to 10.
It's a solid 10. Everywhere.
Which area is the worst?
The thorax, left arm, and lower leg most.
Has it ever spread beyond the wounds?
No, it's concentrated around the shots.
Skin is pink, warm, and clammy with normal turgor. No signs of bruising or scarring previously noted.
No trauma to the head or neck. Pupils are PEARL, reactive, and 3 mm in size. Normocephalic skull with no visible blood pooling.
No signs of airway obstruction or nasal bleed detected upon testing.
Chest symmetry is intact. Clear, equal breath sounds on both sides, with exception noted to the entry wound. No bubbling or air leak from site.
Abdomen soft, non-tender, and non-distended with no guarding, rigidity, or visible exit wound on palpation.

Left upper arm and lower leg injuries were also addressed. Note that the thigh has a severe exit wound with some oozing control since the tourniquet was placed. All other extremities are neurologically intact with sensation and movement to all four limbs.
4-lead ECG was attempted but dislodged due to severe diaphoresis.
VCU Medical Center is the best choice, given the severity. We're transporting there now.
Ready to move. We'll get you onto the stretcher now.
Three-person assist starting now-be ready to stay still.
You're secured. We're heading now to VCU.
Pulse remains steady at 88. No fluctuation during transit detected.
Breathing is at 32 per minute, still elevated but not labored at this point.
BP is unchanged at 160/67 with all catheters and tourniquets in proper position. No hemorrhage noted on back.
Oximetry still at 97% with no need to administer oxygen.
EKG still in sinus rhythm despite movement. No signs of rebleeding identified.
VCU has been briefed; EDR Room 1 is expecting us.
How is the pain now, Jonathan?
Still 10. It's everywhere, but centered around the wounds.
Anything new or different about the pain?
Left leg and chest are still sharp. The rest is dull, aching.
Again, confirm you have no allergies or medications?
No, not that I remember.
IV remains in place and secure at this time with tourniquet also applied appropriately on the left leg. No further procedures needed at this point.
Ready for stretcher loading and transport. Be still.
Transfer to the gurney is complete. Ready for transport to the facility.
All transfers and placements done. On the way now.
VCU has been informed. EDR Room 1 is ready for our arrival.
Patient is safe and secured on the gurney during transport.
All patient findings and current interventions outlined and communicated. Protocol 4-1 followed for general trauma management.
Evaluated as a penetrating trauma case. Protocol 4-1 executed successfully with time-sensitive interventions applied.
Latest vital signs: pulse at 88, respiratory rate 32, and BP still at 160/67. Oxygen saturation remains at 97% on room air.

Breathing condition remains critical but not obstructed. No air leak from wound on lateral inspection and auscultation. Moderate bleeding from left arm and thigh noted. Transport preparations are complete.
